summary: DeVault argues that image hosting websites follow a predictable pattern of decline: they start as simple, useful services, then monetization pressures lead to advertising, anti-user features, and community-building bloat that destroys the original value. He identifies this pattern in PhotoBucket, ImageShack, and TinyPic, and warns that Imgur is following the same trajectory despite its founder's original intentions. DeVault describes his own image hosting service, which runs lean on a single server for $200/month but still loses money. He concludes that profitable image hosting is fundamentally incompatible with maintaining service quality. A 2017 update confirms the thesis: his own service and others shut down due to lack of funds, and he pivoted to a private, invite-only service (sr.ht) with controlled growth.
key_points: Image hosts follow a predictable decline: good service → monetization → advertising → anti-user features → community backlash → failure
Monetization is a poison that creates perverse incentives to add features that degrade the core service
Imgur reached critical mass through Reddit and became immune to the normal user exodus that kills lesser hosts
Running lean and open source can delay but not prevent the fundamental economic problem of free media hosting
The 2017 update vindicates the thesis: multiple services including DeVault's own shut down from lack of funding
money_quotes: First of all, you made an image hosting website. It's already perfect. Almost anything you can think of adding will only make it worse.
You fail, and everyone hates you.
It is impossible to run a profitable image hosting service without sacrificing what makes your service worthwhile.
Your users will be harassed for continuing to use your site.
If you suddenly decide that you need to spend more time on it to justify taking money from it, then you have a lot of time to get things wrong.
We run it incredibly lean - we handle over 10 million HTTP requests per day on a single server that also does transcoding and storage for $200 per month.
key_insight: The economics of free media hosting create an inescapable trap: the moment you try to profit from a simple, useful service, you begin destroying the simplicity that made it useful.
